paper_title: The oxygen isotopic composition of phytolith assemblages from tropical rainforest soil tops

methods.method_name: δ 18 O Silica Measurement and Correction
methods.description: Procedure for preparing phytolith samples using Controlled Isotopic Exchange (CIE), extracting oxygen via IR Laser-Heating Fluorination, analyzing isotopic composition with a dual-inlet mass spectrometer, correcting raw data using standards and an empirical bias correction, validating the correction, and calculating uncertainties using Monte Carlo simulation.

steps.description: Two aliquots of 1.6 mg of the same sample were exchanged with vapor from two waters of a known isotopic composition (Crespin et al., 2008).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform controlled isotopic exchange (CIE) on phytolith samples with known isotopic waters.
inputs: Two waters of known isotopic composition

steps.description: Oxygen extractions were then performed using the IR Laser-Heating Fluorination Technique as described in Alexandre et al. (2006) and Crespin et al. (2008).
steps.searchable_summary: Extract oxygen from exchanged phytolith samples using IR Laser-Heating Fluorination.

steps.description: Oxygen gas samples were directly sent to and analyzed by a dual-inlet mass spectrometer (ThermoQuest Finnigan Delta Plus).
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze oxygen gas isotopic composition using a dual-inlet mass spectrometer.

steps.description: The oxygen isotopic results are expressed in the standard δ-notation relative to V-SMOW.
steps.searchable_summary: Express isotopic results in delta notation relative to V-SMOW.
inputs: Raw isotopic ratios from mass spectrometer
outputs: δ 18 O measured values (relative to V-SMOW)

steps.description: The measured δ 18 O values of each sample (δ 18 O measured 1 , δ 18 O measured 2 ) were corrected on a daily basis using a quartz lab standard (δ 18 O Boulangé 50−100 µm = 16.36 ± 0.09 ‰).
steps.searchable_summary: Correct measured delta O-18 values daily using a quartz lab standard.
inputs: Quartz lab standard δ 18 O value (16.36 ± 0.09 ‰)
outputs: Daily corrected δ 18 O measured values

steps.description: Additionally, we checked that δ 18 O measured 1 and δ 18 O measured 2 values obtained for one to three aliquots of the phytolith lab standard MSG 40 were always in the standard deviation of the mean value measured during a long term calibration (Crespin et al., 2008). During the calibration period, replicate analyses of the international standard NBS 28 gave an average of 9.6 ± 0.17 ‰ (1 SD, n = 13). Replicate analyses of the soil top phytolith samples yielded to a reproducibility for δ 18 O measured better than ±0.5 ‰, except for one sample (#19) (Table 2).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform quality control checks using lab and international standards (MSG 40, NBS 28) and assess sample reproducibility.
inputs: δ 18 O measured values for lab standards (MSG 40, NBS 28) and replicate samples

steps.description: Tests were conducted to examine any possibility of systematic error: before the start of the CIE, the working standards were heated at 350 • C in order to remove possible labile organic remains that may increase the surface area of the materials; the amount of silica subjected to exchange was increased by 6; water-vapour exchange temperature (and associated fractionation factor) was changed; time of vapour-silica exchange was increased while the volume of exchange was reduced by 1/3; the rate of silica dehydration was reduced. None of these tests produced significant changes in the δ 18 O measured values. The difference between the two labelled waters was reduced by 8 ‰, which did not change the δ 18 O silica values either. Additionally, for two working standards (MSG60, BFC), vapour-silica exchanges were carried out at three temperatures (140 • C, 200 • C (usual temperature) and 246 • C). Changes in δ 18 O measured values were conform with those expected from the temperature-dependency of the equilibrium fractionation factors α water-vapour and α vapour-exchanged .
steps.searchable_summary: Investigate potential sources of systematic error and methodological bias by varying experimental parameters.
inputs: Varying experimental parameters (heating temperature, sample amount, exchange temperature, exchange time, exchange volume, dehydration rate, water isotopic difference)
outputs: Assessment of parameter influence on δ 18 O measured values

steps.description: The methodological bias assumed to occur during vapour-silica exchange remains unexplained but was reproducible and could be quantified. For this purpose, pooled values from Chapligin et al. (2012) were taken as true values. CEREGE 1000 ln α vapour-O exchanged values obtained for 140 and 200 • were compared with 1000 ln α vapour-O exchanged values previously obtained for diatoms exchanged at similar temperatures by Labeyrie and Juillet (1982). Difference was invariant with temperature and did not show any relationship with the measured percentage of exchangeable oxygen (R 2 = 0.08) but decreased with increasing δ 18 O silica values (R 2 = 0.4 and 0.9 after outlier removal). The relationship was used in a first step to correct the values of vapour-O exchanged@CEREGE ( vapour-O exchanged was assumed similar to 1000 ln α) as follows: Corrected vapour-Oexchanged@CEREGE = vapour-Oexchanged, Labeyrie and Juillet (1982) − 1.3 × δ 18 O silica@CEREGE + 78. (1)
steps.searchable_summary: Quantify and correct the methodological bias in vapour-O exchanged values using an empirical relationship (Equation 1).
inputs: Published vapour-O exchanged values (Labeyrie and Juillet, 1982)

steps.description: In a second step, the corrected vapour-Oexchanged values were used to correct the δ 18 O silica values.
steps.searchable_summary: Correct delta O-18 silica values using the corrected vapour-O exchanged values.

steps.description: The consistency of this correction was verified using an independent data set previously obtained at CEREGE for fresh water diatoms from Annecy Lake (Crespin et al., 2010). The obtained relationship between diatoms-lake water and temperature of the lake allowed defining a corrected relationship: Corrected diatoms-lake water (‰ vs. VSMOW) with a R 2 of 0.7 and a p-value of 0.002, instead of diatoms-lake water (‰ vs. VSMOW) In a diatoms-lake water vs. t diagram, the obtained corrected fractionation line was shifted towards lower values of diatoms-lake water , close to fractionation lines previously obtained for fresh water diatoms (e.g.Brandriss et al., 1998;Moschen et al., 2005;Dodd and Sharp, 2010).
steps.searchable_summary: Validate the correction procedure using an independent dataset (diatoms) and compare results to published fractionation lines.
inputs: Corrected δ 18 O silica values for independent dataset (diatoms)

steps.description: For each of the samples, mean and standard deviation (SD) of X (exchangeable oxygen), δ 18 O silica , and corrected δ 18 O silica were calculated using R and a Monte Carlo simulation: X, δ 18 O silica and corrected δ 18 O silica were computed 10 000 times, using 10 000 simulated values of the variables taken into account in the equations (Crespin et al., 2008 and this section). The simulated uncertainty (SD) on corrected δ 18 O silica ranged from ±0.5 to 1.2 ‰ for 15 over 16 samples and reached ±2.7 ‰ for one sample (#38; Table 2).
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate mean and standard deviation for exchangeable oxygen, delta O-18 silica, and corrected delta O-18 silica using Monte Carlo simulation in R.
inputs: Equations used for calculation (Crespin et al., 2008 and this section)
outputs: Mean and standard deviation for X
Mean and standard deviation for δ 18 O silica
Mean and standard deviation for corrected δ 18 O silica
